data.title: Markets get NACHO instead of TACO
data.description:
President Donald Trump has reportedly told aides to prepare for an extended blockade of Iran’s port.

Trump also posted overnight that Iran should “Get smart soon!”

Markets are “unlikely to give too much weight to this,” UBS Chief Economist Paul Donovan wrote. “U.S. political pressures seem to be increasing — Trump’s approval rating hit an all-time low in the Reuters/Ipsos poll; approval of the president’s handling of the cost of living was 22%.”

But Bloomberg columnist Javier Blas said that, according to one trader, the market narrative has shifted to NACHO from TACO.

“We thought we were getting a TACO — ‘Trump Always Chickens Out.’ But so far we are getting a NACHO — ‘Not A Chance Hormuz Opens.’”

Oil continued its rise this morning, with WTI (CL1:COM) topping $103/bbl and Brent (CO1:COM) topping $114/bbl.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Safe Pro Group signs US defense AI deal; stock takes 6% hit in premarket
data.description:
• Safe Pro Group (SPAI) announced on Wednesday that it has signed a master teaming agreement with a U.S. defense prime contractor to expand AI-based defense and autonomy solutions for government use.
  • The partnership strengthens Safe Pro's position in defense AI by integrating its SPOTD threat-detection system into larger military drone and autonomous platform programs.
  • SPOTD can analyze drone data to detect and map 150+ landmines and explosive threats, turning video into usable battlefield intelligence.
  • The deal builds on an earlier $1.0M U.S. government subcontract and could open doors to larger defense contracts through the prime contractor pipeline.
  • Following the news, the stock plunged ~6.35% to ~$3.98 in premarket hours.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Yimutian plans 15-for-1 ADS reverse split after Nasdaq bid price notice
data.description:
• Yimutian (YMT) on Wednesday said it plans to change the ratio of its American depositary shares (ADS) to ordinary shares, weeks after receiving a notice from Nasdaq over minimum bid price deficiency.
  • The company will adjust the ADS-to-Class A ordinary share ratio from one ADS representing 25 shares to one ADS representing 375 shares, effective on or around May 18. 
  • The move is equivalent to a 15-for-1 reverse split for ADS holders. JPMorgan Chase Bank is acting as depositary.
  • The change follows an April 2 notice from Nasdaq stating that Yimutian had fallen below the $1 minimum bid price requirement after its shares traded under that level between Feb. 18 and April 1.
  • Yimutian said the ratio change will not affect its underlying ordinary shares, and no shares will be issued or canceled. 
  • Shares down -11% premarket

data.title: CTS Corporation Non-GAAP EPS of $0.62 beats by $0.10, revenue of $139M beats by $2.17M
data.description:
• CTS Corporation press release (CTS): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.62 beats by $0.10.
  • Revenue of $139M (+10.3% Y/Y) beats by $2.17M.
  • Sales were $139 million in the first quarter of 2026, up 11% year-over-year. Sales to diversified end-markets increased 18%. Sales to the transportation market increased 3%. 
  • Assuming the continuation of current market conditions, CTS is narrowing its previous guidance of 2026 sales from a range of $550-$580 million to $560-$580 million and adjusted diluted EPS from a range of $2.30-$2.45 to $2.35-$2.45.

data.title: Earnings Snapshot: AbbVie beats Q1 revenue estimates, misses on profit; raises FY26 outlook
ASE outlines Q2 2026 revenue growth of 7%-9% while projecting LEAP revenue over $3.5B
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: ASE Technology Holding Co., Ltd. (ASX) Q1 2026

Management view

  • Kenneth Hsiang (Head of Investor Relations & Senior VP) said the quarter showed a split demand pattern, with “seasonality in our EMS business, while demand for our ATM services did not slow at all,” adding that “even with less working days during the first quarter, our ATM revenues grew sequentially.”

  • Kenneth Hsiang (Head of Investor Relations & Senior VP) framed capacity as a binding constraint, saying “our capacities along with those of our upstream foundry partners are finite with limited ability to be pulled forward at this point,” while also noting “we have been installing additional LEAP manufacturing capacities that are expected to start generating revenues weighted towards the fourth quarter.”

  • Joseph Tung (Group Chief Financial Officer) raised the company’s investment plan, stating, “we are upping our CapEx for the year,” including “additional [ NTD 0.9 billion ] for buildings and infrastructure” and “an incremental USD 0.6 billion in machinery,” driven by “stronger demand for LEAP services this year and next.”

  • Joseph Tung (Group Chief Financial Officer) lifted the LEAP revenue outlook and tied it to pricing, saying, “for ATM 2026 revenue, we now expect LEAP services revenue to be around 10% above our prior guidance, reaching over USD 3.5 billion,” and added, “our strong market position continues to support a favorable pricing environment throughout the year.”

Outlook

  • Joseph Tung (Group Chief Financial Officer) guided Q2 2026 at the consolidated level to “revenue should grow by 7% to 9% quarter-over-quarter,” with “gross margin should increase by 20 to 100 basis points” and “operating margin should increase by 50 to 120 basis points quarter-over-quarter.”

  • Joseph Tung (Group Chief Financial Officer) guided the ATM segment to “revenue should grow by 9% to 11% quarter-over-quarter” and “gross margin should be between 26% to 27%.”

  • Joseph Tung (Group Chief Financial Officer) guided EMS to “revenue should grow at least 10% year-over-year” and said “EMS operating margin should be similar with second quarter 2025 levels.”

  • Compared with the prior call’s emphasis on expected Q1 seasonality (including an ATM Q1 gross margin guide of “24% to 25%”), management’s Q1 2026 commentary highlighted upside to margin and demand resilience, with Tung stating Q1 ATM gross margin “is ahead of our original expectation of 24.5%.”

Financial results

  • Kenneth Hsiang (Head of Investor Relations & Senior VP) reported Q1 2026 fully diluted EPS of NTD 3.08 and consolidated net revenues of NTD 173.7 billion, alongside gross margin of 20.1% and operating margin of 10.1%.

  • Kenneth Hsiang (Head of Investor Relations & Senior VP) said Q1 ATM revenue was a record NTD 112.4 billion, and characterized the quarter as avoiding a typical seasonal decline, stating the business “was able to avoid a seasonal decline in revenue during the first quarter upsiding our original expectations.”

  • Kenneth Hsiang (Head of Investor Relations & Senior VP) highlighted the LEAP investment ramp effects on reported profitability, saying “we will continue to see gradually increasing depreciation without significant amounts of associated revenue during the tuning and qualification period,” while adding that “revenues associated with these full process lines will ramp mostly during the fourth quarter.”

  • Kenneth Hsiang (Head of Investor Relations & Senior VP) reported Q1 balance sheet and funding capacity items including cash, cash equivalents and current financial assets of NTD 114 billion, total interest-bearing debt of NTD 265.3 billion, and “total unused credit lines amounted to NTD 419.4 billion.”

Q&A

  • Gokul Hariharan, JPMorgan Chase & Co.: Asked what drove the “10% upside in LEAP in 2026” and whether 2027 implies “NTD 2 billion plus” incremental LEAP revenue; Joseph Tung (Group Chief Financial Officer) said demand is “stronger than anticipated,” described mix as “about 75% in assembly and 25% in tests,” and for 2027 said, “we can safely expect even stronger momentum than that, although I'm not giving out any particular number at this point.”

  • Gokul Hariharan, JPMorgan Chase & Co.: Pressed on full-process progress and AI-accelerator relevance; Joseph Tung (Group Chief Financial Officer) said, “we still believe that we can reach this NTD 300 million revenue mark for this year,” and added the company is “expanding our customer base in this particular area.”

  • Sunny Lin, UBS Investment Bank: Asked about CPO role and timing; Joseph Tung (Group Chief Financial Officer) said, “we are working very closely with the foundry as well as our customer,” and on timing/details added, “I don't have that much of a detail, except we will start with the packaging part of it.”

  • Charlie Chan, Morgan Stanley: Asked about smartphone/PC demand and pricing power amid cost volatility; Joseph Tung (Group Chief Financial Officer) said “the PC and cellphone market softness continues,” but demand is being offset by “AI peripheral chips,” and on pricing stated, “Whatever the cost increase there is because of the market uncertainties, those can be fully passed through.”

  • Zheng Lu, Goldman Sachs Group: Asked about investment outside Taiwan and full-process profitability; Joseph Tung (Group Chief Financial Officer) pointed to “Malaysia” via an “acquired a factory from ADI” and said Singapore test is ramping “to serve the AI-related test requirement,” while on profitability said, “in theory, it should be accretive to LEAP.”

  • Rick Hsu, Daiwa Securities: Asked whether Q1 strength reflected pull-ins and risk of second-half rebalancing; Joseph Tung (Group Chief Financial Officer) said, “we don't have the capacity to entertain those pull-in demand,” and added, “I don't think it's because of the customers' early pull-in that really bumped up our revenue for the quarter.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was slightly positive, with repeated congratulations paired with probing on durability and constraints, including questions on whether this is “a peak of CapEx,” potential “free cash flow negative,” pricing leverage, and whether demand strength reflects “order pull in.”

  • Management sentiment was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more cautious under follow-ups, repeatedly signaling momentum while limiting specificity; Joseph Tung (Group Chief Financial Officer) said “things are moving very fast,” declined to raise structural margin now, and on CapEx added, “I wouldn't preclude that there will be a further need for us to increase our CapEx for the year.”

  • Versus the previous quarter’s tone (which included longer-term framing and broader strategic narration from Tien Wu), the current call leaned more execution- and constraint-focused, with more explicit discussion of depreciation timing, facilities as gating factors, and funding via borrowing.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Leadership presence changed in the prepared section: Q4 2025 included keynote remarks from Tien Wu (Group COO & Representative Director), while Q1 2026’s prepared remarks were delivered by Kenneth Hsiang and guidance by Joseph Tung.

  • Guidance language shifted from Q4’s expectation of “much stronger than normal seasonality” in Q1 and an ATM gross margin guide of “24% to 25%,” to Q1’s reported resilience and a Q2 ATM gross margin guide of “26% to 27%,” alongside an increased full-year LEAP revenue outlook to “over USD 3.5 billion.”

  • Analyst focus remained heavily centered on LEAP, full process, and capacity, but Q1 2026 added more direct questioning on funding, potential further CapEx increases, and whether tight equipment supply elongates lead times.

Risks and concerns

  • Kenneth Hsiang (Head of Investor Relations & Senior VP) described operational transition risk, saying blended utilization “can be slightly misleading” due to LEAP capacity installations and consolidation of traditional capacity.

  • Management flagged margin pressure from ramp timing and fixed-cost absorption: Kenneth Hsiang (Head of Investor Relations & Senior VP) said depreciation can rise “without significant amounts of associated revenue during the tuning and qualification period,” and Joseph Tung (Group Chief Financial Officer) said Q2 improvement “will be partly offset by higher costs associated with early resource deployment for product transitions.”

  • Funding and cash flow sensitivity surfaced in Q&A, with Joseph Tung (Group Chief Financial Officer) stating, “the funding gap will mostly be funded by additional borrowing,” and separately acknowledging, “next year could be another CapEx heavy year for us.”

Final takeaway

Management characterized Q1 as an ATM-led quarter with record ATM revenue and margin upside versus internal expectations, while raising full-year investment plans and lifting the LEAP revenue outlook to “over USD 3.5 billion.” Guidance called for Q2 sequential improvement across revenue and margins, but executives repeatedly emphasized capacity, facilities, and ramp-related depreciation as near-term constraints, with incremental funding needs expected to be covered “mostly” through additional borrowing.
